dialogue.utterance: Unit 3 respond for possible CVA in Richmond.
Hello, I'm Morgan, an EMT. What brought you to call 911 tonight?
I'm having trouble walking, speaking, and I think it's a stroke.
She's been having trouble since about 8:30. Unsteady gait, trouble swallowing, and aphasia.
Ma'am, can you tell us your name?
Patricia Smith.
What city are we in right now?
Richmond.
Do you know the date today?
Today is May 10th.
Ma'am, why did you call 911?
Because I can't move my left side well and I'm having trouble speaking clearly.
Ma'am, your airway is patent and you are maintaining it independently.
You have regular respirations at 16 breaths per minute.
Your blood pressure is 142/75 mmHg.
Your pulse is at 59 beats per minute and strong.
Your oxygen saturation is 98% on room air.
Your breathing is not labored and you don't look in distress.
Your current blood glucose is 119 mg/dL, which is normal for the moment.
Ma'am, your skin is warm and dry. Can we check your pulse in both arms with your permission?
Yes, but my right leg feels a bit heavy.
Let's go over what's happened since the symptoms started. Tell us what you've noticed.
I started noticing some left-side weakness, which has gotten worse.
This morning, her blood sugar was low-around 60. She drank orange juice and it increased to 130.
What else happened in the evening time?
Things got worse around 8:30. I'm having issues walking and my speech is difficult now.
Have you been struggling with urinating at all tonight?
Yes, it's painful and hard to do today.
On a scale from 0 to 10, how much does this pain hurt right now?
It's about a 2 for me right now.
Does that pain spread to any other parts of your body?
It's mostly in my back and the left side of my body.
Do you have a history of strokes, Ma'am?
She's had four strokes before, all with weakness in the left side of the body.
Are you familiar with conditions like hypertension or diabetes?
Yes, she has type 2 diabetes and high blood pressure as medical conditions.
Ma'am, can we place you on a cardiac monitor for monitoring your heart rhythm?
Yes, please do that.
Electrodes are now applied. We have 4-lead monitoring running now.
The EKG shows sinus bradycardia with a regular rhythm pattern.
Ma'am, your speech is now clearer, and your facial features are symmetrical at the time of assessment.
Throat and mouth look unremarkable-no sores or drainage for us to find now.
Skin is still warm and dry. Are you noticing any edema in your limbs today?
Yes, swelling in my lower legs is something I've noticed today as well.
We'll now perform a breath sound auscultation in your chest area.
Breath sounds are clear and symmetrical in both lungs without any adventitious sounds detected.
Your abdomen is soft and non-tender with no discomfort mentioned at this time.
You mentioned swelling and pain-can you point out your right side pain area now, please?
Yes, this pain is in my right upper back area predominantly for now.
We'll now start an intravenous line to support intravenous access during transport.
The IV has been placed in the right antecubital vein and is secured properly now.
We've obtained a 12-lead EKG and it shows nonspecific T-wave changes and sinus bradycardia for the 40+ female patient.
At 21:57, your pulse is 55, SpO2 is 95%, and your blood pressure is now 154/77 mmHg.
We will now activate the stroke protocol based on your symptoms and current EKG findings.
At 22:06, your pulse has dropped to 57 and blood pressure is 131/68 with SpO2 at 96% on room air.
At 22:15, pulse is back to 55, SpO2 is 98%, blood pressure is 132/63 today, and EKG is still sinus bradycardia without change.
Ma'am, where would you like to be transported for emergency care if able to decide at this time?
St. Mary's Hospital in Richmond is where I'd prefer my care continues.
We will now secure you on the stretcher using three safety belts for safe movement up steps safely at the entrance.
All three safety belts have been properly fastened and are now securing the patient safely for transport in this situation.
We noticed that the address has four front steps, so we'll carry you carefully while maintaining your safety during movement phases later.
Once on the stretcher, we'll fully secure you using five safety straps which is the usual protocol for safe transport purposes.
The stretcher is now secured inside our transport vehicle for safe transport to the hospital ahead of us today in the task here.
You remain alert and oriented in all four domains today. Your GCS is 15 as noted in this clinical record.
Patient is loaded into the ambulance with stretcher secured in place as required for safe travel on the way now towards the hospital for further evaluation and management of this CVA case today.
We do not have repeat glucose testing due to the unit's current documented findings.
Ma'am, you are alert and oriented to person, place, time, and events (AOx4). This helps our evaluation.
Given your current state and documented EKG findings, your field impressions are consistent with stroke and possible anxiety.
Ma'am, we will continue continuous cardiac monitoring during transit, as per our protocol.
All straps are double-checked and everything is safe to proceed for this patient as per protocol while en route and while monitoring your condition carefully during transport.
We are now en route to St. Mary's with all vitals stable and no signs of deterioration en route.

We are now at St. Mary's and are being met by the stroke team that includes an ED staff and MD, as per report given in the documentation now for patient transfer and further management according to protocol today for stroke care.

We've completed and signed the full report with ED staff including the ED physician today as part of our handover process now for safe care transitions and hospital discharge as per the EMS protocol currently in use for the stroke case.
